full_response:
Let  

A = 𝑨 = [ [ 1 , 2 ] ; [ −1 , 2 ] ],   b⃗ = ( 1 , 3 )ᵀ ,   x⃗ = ( x₁ , x₂ )ᵀ.  

Bob’s objective function is  

f_{A,b}(x⃗) = ½ x⃗ᵀ (Aᵀ A) x⃗ − b⃗ᵀ A x⃗.                                 (1)

A critical point is a point where the gradient ∇f_{A,b} is zero (the gradient exists everywhere here, so “undefined” never happens).

Step 1.  Compute Aᵀ A.  
Aᵀ = [ [ 1 , −1 ] ; [ 2 , 2 ] ]

Aᵀ A = [ [ 1·1 + (−1)(−1) , 1·2 + (−1)·2 ]  
          [ 2·1 + 2(−1)     , 2·2 + 2·2       ] ]  
      = [ [ 2 , 0 ] ; [ 0 , 8 ] ].                        (2)

Step 2.  Compute Aᵀ b⃗.  
Aᵀ b⃗ = [ [ 1 , −1 ] ; [ 2 , 2 ] ] ( 1 , 3 )ᵀ  
        = ( 1·1 + (−1)·3 , 2·1 + 2·3 )ᵀ  
        = ( −2 , 8 )ᵀ.                                (3)

Step 3.  Compute the gradient.  
Using (1) and the fact that ∇(½ xᵀ M x) = M x for any constant matrix M,

∇f_{A,b}(x⃗) = (Aᵀ A) x⃗ − Aᵀ b⃗.                       (4)

Insert (2) and (3):

∇f_{A,b}(x⃗) =
[ [ 2 , 0 ] ; [ 0 , 8 ] ] ( x₁ , x₂ )ᵀ − ( −2 , 8 )ᵀ
= ( 2x₁ , 8x₂ )ᵀ − ( −2 , 8 )ᵀ
= ( 2x₁ + 2 , 8x₂ − 8 )ᵀ.                       (5)

Step 4.  Solve ∇f_{A,b}(x⃗) = 0⃗.  
Set each component of (5) to zero:

2x₁ + 2 = 0 ⇒ x₁ = −1,  
8x₂ − 8 = 0 ⇒ x₂ = 1.                          (6)

Step 5.  Conclude.  
Because f_{A,b} is a quadratic function with a everywhere-defined gradient, every critical point must satisfy (6). Hence

x₁ = −1 and x₂ = 1

for any critical point.  But (−1, 1) is exactly the (unique) solution of the linear system A x⃗ = b⃗:

A ( −1 , 1 )ᵀ = ( 1·(−1)+2·1 , −1·(−1)+2·1 )ᵀ = ( 1 , 3 )ᵀ = b⃗.

Thus all critical points coincide with the solution of the system.

\boxed{\text{Any critical point of }f_{A,b}(x⃗)\text{ satisfies }x₁=-1,\;x₂=1.}  

Confidence: 0.19
